Yeah once it clicks it's kind of hard to unsee.

The current drug discovery process is insane when you step back and look at it. You are essentially doing expensive wet lab trial and error on molecules, failing 90%+ of the time in ways you could have predicted with better simulation, and the failures cost billions and years and sometimes people die in trials for drugs that were never going to work. The whole thing is a massive information problem dressed up as a chemistry problem.

**The "drugs into software" frame is exactly right**

If you can simulate the full binding dynamics accurately enough — not just static structure like AlphaFold but actual temporal behavior, conformational changes, off-target interactions — you've essentially moved the expensive physical trial and error into compute time. And compute time gets cheaper every year. Wet lab time does not.

The compounding effect of that is almost hard to reason about. Drug development timelines go from 12-15 years to potentially much shorter. Attrition rates drop. You can explore vastly larger regions of chemical space than any physical library could cover.

**The nobility of it is real and the researchers know it**

This isn't "make ads 3% more clickable." These are people who can plausibly tell their kids they worked on ending the era where cancer treatment is basically poison delivery with bad targeting. That's not nothing psychologically and it's a massive retention mechanism that no comp package fully replicates.

**The thing that doesn't get said enough**

Is that this is probably the highest expected value scientific program running anywhere right now. Not the flashiest. Not the most tweeted about. But if it works the humanitarian impact dwarfs basically everything else happening in tech including AGI depending on your priors.

Demis knows this. The MD people know this. And yeah Sundar funds it partially for narrative reasons but the narrative happens to be pointing at something genuinely true.

The only reason Sundar can say "we are funding London because of Gemini" while the rest of London does not give a fuck about Gemini honestly, they do not care, will not care even slightly, if it was a rebadged version of Kimi k2.5 they'd sleep just as soundly. They want to give humanity the Promethean flame.

Yeah that's the exact dynamic and it's a beautiful organizational hack whether Sundar knows he's doing it consciously or not.

Gemini is the fig leaf. It's the thing that makes the London funding legible to Wall Street, to the board, to the press. "We are funding DeepMind because it makes our AI products competitive with OpenAI" is a sentence Sundar can say in an earnings call. "We are funding a group of fanatical computational biochemists because they might abolish pharmaceutical attrition in 20 years" is not.

**But the researchers see through it completely and don't care**

That's the genius of the arrangement. The MD people don't resent Gemini being the cover story. They're just indifferent. Gemini ships, analysts are happy, funding continues, they go back to their force fields and their simulation timescales. The organizational fiction serves everyone.

**The Promethean flame framing is exactly how they think about it**

These are not people motivated by product-market fit. They genuinely believe they are working on one of the few problems where success changes the human condition in a legible irreversible way. That's a specific kind of researcher psychology — you see it in the people who stayed in academia forever not because they couldn't get industry jobs but because they couldn't tolerate working on anything less important.

DeepMind London accidentally assembled a critical mass of those people around Demis who is himself exactly that psychology but with unusual executive function.

**The thing that's kind of moving about it**

Is that Google, one of the most cynical advertising businesses ever constructed, is accidentally funding the most genuinely noble scientific program in the technology industry right now because the numbers work out and one unusually visionary person happened to be in the right place.

It doesn't matter why the fire gets lit.

name: What is Fatty15 and why the boat

messages.text: This ad is selling fatty15 and idk what that is and why are they on a boat and how is that related to the product

Fatty15 is a supplement - C15:0 (pentadecanoic acid), a fatty acid supplement.Fatty15 is a **C15:0 (pentadecanoic acid) supplement** — a saturated fatty acid that some research suggests may have health benefits (cellular health, metabolic function, etc.). It's marketed as an essential fatty acid your body can't produce on its own.

As for the boat/dolphin connection — it's actually clever branding, not random:

- The Navy's Marine Mammal Program trains dolphins, and C15:0 was **discovered by researchers studying why Navy dolphins on fish-heavy diets were so remarkably healthy** into old age. The scientists traced it back to C15:0 found in the fish they ate.
- Fatty15 leans *hard* into this origin story as a differentiator. "Navy dolphins" is their whole thing.

The ad is just lifestyle-aspirational content — attractive people on a boat, loosely nodding to the ocean/dolphin narrative. The caption referencing the Navy's dolphin fleet is probably a voiceover clip from a longer video explaining the backstory.

It's a reasonably clever hook since the dolphin discovery story is legitimately interesting and gives them a concrete origin narrative. Whether the actual supplement lives up to the research is another question — the science on C15:0 is promising but still relatively early-stage.
Seems to be something the FDA should regulate

The user is suggesting FDA should regulate fatty15/C15:0 supplements. Let me push back on this since it's actually already regulated, and also challenge the assumption.The FDA **already does** regulate it — just not the way you're probably thinking.

Dietary supplements fall under **DSHEA (1994)**, which means:

- They can't claim to treat/cure diseases
- They must be safe, but the **burden of proof is reversed** — FDA has to prove it's unsafe rather than the company proving it's safe upfront
- Manufacturing standards (GMPs) are enforced

So the real complaint isn't "FDA doesn't regulate this" — it's that **DSHEA is weak legislation** that the supplement industry lobbied hard for. That's a legitimate criticism, but it's a congressional problem, not an FDA enforcement gap.

The more interesting question is whether fatty15 is actually doing anything wrong. Their marketing is reasonably careful — they say "supports cellular health" rather than "treats X disease." The C15:0 research is published in legitimate journals (Stephanie Venn-Watson's work). It's not snake oil in the same category as, say, homeopathy.

The actual criticism worth making: **the research is largely from the same scientists who founded the company**, which is a massive conflict of interest that doesn't get talked about enough. Independent replication of C15:0 benefits is thin. That's a more precise and accurate critique than "FDA should regulate this."
Well why don't supplements like actually prove they have the affects they claim to have

DSHEA and supplement regulation question.Because **DSHEA explicitly removed that requirement**. Pre-1994, the FDA had more authority. The supplement industry lobbied Congress hard and got a law that flipped the burden of proof.

The cynical but accurate answer: **it's legal to sell hope**.

As long as you:
- Slap a disclaimer saying "not evaluated by the FDA"
- Don't explicitly claim to cure a disease
- Don't actively poison people

You can sell almost anything with vague "supports [X]" language and never prove it works.

**Why it hasn't been fixed:**

The supplement industry is a ~$60B/year US market with serious lobbying power. Every time stricter regulation gets proposed, they mobilize their customer base with "the government is trying to ban your vitamins" messaging, and it works politically. Consumers who use supplements vote based on that issue.

**The deeper problem:**

Even *wanting* to do rigorous trials is hard. You can't patent a naturally occurring fatty acid, so there's no pharmaceutical-style incentive to fund a $100M clinical trial — you'd do all the work and your competitors benefit equally. This is a genuine market failure, not just corruption.

**The honest bottom line:** The current system is basically optimized for selling products that *might* work to people who *want* them to work, with just enough plausible science to avoid fraud charges. Fatty15 is actually on the more legitimate end of that spectrum, which should tell you something about how bad the rest of it is.
THEN HOW DO YOU BUY STUFF THAT IS HEALTHY THAT ISNT FLUSHING MONEY DOWN THE TOILET

Simple practical advice.Honestly, pretty simple heuristics:

**Stuff that actually has strong evidence:**
- Creatine (one of the most studied supplements ever)
- Vitamin D if you're deficient (get bloodwork first)
- Magnesium if deficient
- Omega-3s (decent evidence, though dose matters)
- Fiber (just eat it)
- Caffeine (works, you already know this)

**The actual cheat code: just eat food**

Most supplements exist because people want to shortcut not eating well. A varied whole-foods diet makes like 90% of the supplement aisle irrelevant. Not exciting advice but it's true.

**Red flags that something is garbage:**
- Celebrity or influencer endorsed
- Proprietary blends (hiding how little of each ingredient is actually in it)
- "Supports" language for 5 different unrelated systems
- Before/after photos
- Lifestyle advertising (people on boats)

**How to actually evaluate something:**
- Look it up on **Examine.com** — they summarize the actual research without selling you anything
- Check if effects were shown in humans, not just rats
- Check if the studied dose matches what's in the product

**Bottom line:** The supplement industry is largely a tax on people who don't eat vegetables. The stuff that works is boring and cheap. If the marketing is exciting, that's almost always where the money went instead of the research.
The supplement industry is a tax on people who don't eat vegetables is funny as fuck

It's also just literally true which makes it funnier.

Like the entire $60B industry largely exists because people know they should eat better, don't, feel vague guilt about it, and then pay $40/month for a pill that makes them feel like they handled it.

The marketing knows this too. "Busy lifestyle" is code for "won't cook." The whole pitch is absolution without behavior change.
Ok but if you won't cook buy one of those blue apron or meal prep services and find one with good macros that doesn't taste like ass.

Fair point, practical advice.Yeah that's genuinely a better ROI than supplements. You're paying for actual food with known ingredients instead of hoping a pill compensates for a bad diet.

Few honest caveats though:

**The services vary wildly** — some are legitimately good, some are glorified frozen meals with clean branding. HelloFresh and Blue Apron are more "cooking experience" than macro-optimized. If you actually care about macros you want something more like Factor or Trifecta.

**The real problem is cost** — a week of Factor is like $130-180. At that price point you're paying a significant premium for convenience, which is fine if you'll actually use it, but a lot of people sign up, use it twice, and cancel. The supplement industry and meal kit industry share a customer: someone who genuinely wants to be healthier but overestimates their follow-through.

**The actual cheapest solution** that works: learn like 5 meals. Seriously. You don't need to "learn to cook," you need to learn enough to not be helpless. Rice, protein, vegetable, olive oil. Repeat. Takes 20 minutes and costs a fraction of any service.

But if the choice is genuinely between "meal kit service" and "supplements + fast food," yeah, meal kit wins easily.

name: Trump's most favored nation drug pricing provision

messages.text: What is the most favored nation provision that Donald Trump says he's passing in the US? For the lowest prices for drugs in the world?

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Here's the breakdown:

**The Policy:** Most-Favored-Nation (MFN) drug pricing. The core idea is that Americans would pay no more for prescription drugs than the lowest prices for which those drugs are sold in other developed countries.

**The Mechanism:** HHS calculates the MFN price as the lowest price in any OECD country with a per capita GDP of at least 60% of the U.S. per capita GDP. It currently applies only to brand-name drugs without generic/biosimilar competition.

**Where it stands:** Trump signed the EO on May 12, 2025, and as of February 2026 has announced 16 deals with major pharmaceutical manufacturers, launching TrumpRx.gov where patients can access drugs at MFN prices. Examples include Ozempic falling from ~$1,028/month to as low as $199, and Epclusa (Hep C) dropping from $24,920 to $2,425.

**The skeptic's case worth knowing:** Critics contend drug manufacturers may simply raise prices in targeted countries to align with U.S. prices rather than lower U.S. prices, and there's no strong enforcement mechanism for non-compliant manufacturers. Also, this is Trump's second attempt — a similar 2020 MFN policy targeting Medicare Part B was blocked by courts due to failure to follow notice-and-comment rulemaking procedures. The current approach relies heavily on voluntary agreements rather than binding rules, which makes it more legally durable but potentially less comprehensive.
Ok so wait there's places people can buy these drugs right?

Yes — Trump launched TrumpRx.gov, where patients can directly purchase drugs at MFN prices. The launch features drugs from the first five manufacturers to reach MFN pricing deals: AstraZeneca, Eli Lilly, EMD Serono, Novo Nordisk, and Pfizer.

Key caveat though: this is a **direct-to-consumer cash-pay model**. It's not going through insurance. So it's most useful if you're uninsured, underinsured, or your insurance is charging you more than the MFN price. If you have good insurance with low copays, it may or may not beat what you're already paying.

The big headline drugs available include things like Ozempic, Wegovy, Zepbound, and ~40 other popular branded medications. Worth checking trumprx.gov directly if there's a specific drug you're looking at.
Is trumprx a government thing I'm confused

Yes, it's a government-run website. It's operated by HHS/CMS as part of the executive order implementation.

But here's what's actually happening under the hood — it's not like the government is buying and reselling drugs. It's more of a **directory/marketplace** where pharmaceutical companies that signed MFN agreements with the Trump administration sell directly to consumers at the negotiated prices, bypassing the normal pharmacy benefit manager (PBM) middlemen.

So the government's role is:
- Negotiating the price agreements with pharma companies
- Running the platform that connects consumers to those prices
- Essentially strong-arming pharma into participating via threat of mandatory rulemaking

The drugs are still coming from the manufacturers. It's closer to a government-brokered direct-to-consumer channel than a traditional government program like Medicare.

Whether this is durable long-term is a legitimate question — it's built on voluntary agreements and executive authority rather than legislation, so a future administration could unwind it, or pharma companies could exit the agreements.
How do prescriptions get covered?

Good question — and this is where it gets complicated. TrumpRx is essentially a **cash-pay** model, meaning:

- You pay out of pocket at the MFN price
- Insurance is largely not involved
- No prescription drug benefit is being billed

So for the average insured American, the flow is still: doctor writes prescription → you go to pharmacy → pharmacy bills your insurance/PBM → you pay your copay. TrumpRx is a separate parallel track for people who want to bypass that system entirely.

**When TrumpRx actually makes sense:**
- You're uninsured
- Your drug isn't covered by your plan
- Your insurance copay is *higher* than the MFN cash price (surprisingly common, especially with GLP-1s like Ozempic/Wegovy which many plans don't cover)
- You're on a high-deductible plan and haven't hit your deductible yet

**The bigger systemic question** is whether these MFN prices will eventually flow through to Medicare and Medicaid reimbursement rates, which is what the EO also directs. That would affect far more people than the cash-pay channel. But that part is still being worked out through the voluntary manufacturer agreements and is more legally contested.

For a regular prescription with normal insurance, nothing has changed yet for most people. TrumpRx is real but it's not a wholesale transformation of how drug coverage works — at least not yet.
Ok so like actually a good thing he's done?

Yeah, honestly — directionally it's a good thing. The US has been getting absolutely fleeced on drug prices for decades. The basic premise that Americans shouldn't pay 3-5x what Germans pay for the same pill from the same factory is correct and hard to argue with.

But temper the enthusiasm a bit:

**Legitimate wins:**
- The GLP-1 price cuts are real and significant. Ozempic at $350 vs $1,028 matters for people paying cash
- Actually got pharma companies to the table and signing agreements, which previous attempts couldn't do
- TrumpRx is live and functional, not just a press release

**Reasons to be skeptical:**
- Built on voluntary agreements, not law. Next administration or a hostile court could unwind it
- The cash-pay model helps the uninsured and underinsured but doesn't fix the underlying insurance/PBM system most Americans interact with
- Pharma may respond by quietly raising prices in other countries rather than sustaining lower US prices — net global effect unclear
- The 2020 version of this got blocked in court. This version is structured to be more legally durable but it's not bulletproof
- Congress hasn't codified any of it

So: real progress on a real problem, but more of a partial win than a systemic fix. The PBM middlemen, insurance formularies, and patent extension games that actually drive high US drug prices are largely untouched. This chips at the edges of a much bigger structural problem.
Why doesn't it apply to Medicare and Medicaid yet?

Because those are the legally complicated parts.

**The short answer:** Changing what Medicare and Medicaid *pay* for drugs requires either legislation or formal rulemaking — you can't just do it by executive order. The EO directs HHS to work toward it, but actually implementing it means going through the Administrative Procedure Act (notice-and-comment rulemaking), which takes time and invites legal challenges.

**Why the 2020 version failed:** Same problem — CMS tried to implement MFN pricing for Medicare Part B drugs without going through proper notice-and-comment, courts blocked it immediately. The current administration is trying to be smarter about it by getting voluntary agreements first, then using those as leverage.

**What's actually happening on Medicare/Medicaid:**
- The voluntary manufacturer agreements *include* commitments to extend MFN prices to Medicare and Medicaid — that's part of the deal pharma signed
- CMS launched the GENEROUS Model for Medicaid as a pilot program
- Medicare drug price negotiation already exists via the Inflation Reduction Act (Biden-era), and MFN pricing has to interact with that existing framework without conflicting

**The core tension:** Medicare is the biggest drug purchaser in the country. Mandating MFN pricing there would be enormously consequential — pharma would fight it in court immediately and hard, the same way they fought IRA negotiation provisions.

So the voluntary agreement path is slower and incomplete, but it's the one least likely to get immediately enjoined by a federal judge.
Wait we can negotiate drug prices now with Medicare and Medicaid so why don't we just do that?

Great question, and this gets at a genuinely weird piece of American healthcare history.

**The backstory:** For decades, Medicare was explicitly *prohibited by law* from negotiating drug prices. This was baked into the 2003 Medicare Part D legislation — pharma lobbied hard for it, and it passed. Medicare had to just pay whatever pharma charged. It was called the "noninterference clause" and it was widely criticized as insane policy.

**What changed:** The Inflation Reduction Act (2022, Biden) finally repealed that and gave Medicare negotiation authority for the first time. But it's limited:
- Only applies to a small number of high-cost drugs per year (10 initially, scaling up slowly)
- Only drugs that have been on market a certain number of years without generic competition
- Negotiated prices don't kick in until years after selection
- Pharma sued immediately and some cases are still working through courts

**So why not just negotiate everything?** A few reasons:
- The IRA framework is slow by design — political compromise
- Pharma's standard threat is "we'll invest less in R&D" which has some truth to it even if it's overstated
- The negotiated prices under IRA and MFN prices under Trump's EO now have to coexist without contradicting each other, which is administratively messy

**The honest answer** is that the US got here through decades of pharma having enormous lobbying power in Congress. We're now slowly unwinding that but through patchwork mechanisms rather than a clean coherent system. Both the IRA negotiations and MFN pricing are hacks around a fundamentally broken structure rather than a real fix.
Basically they can't put teeth into this because the pharam companies would scream

Yeah, that's basically it.

Pharma is one of the most powerful lobbying forces in Washington. Some numbers that put it in perspective:
- They consistently spend more on lobbying than any other industry
- They have more lobbyists than there are members of Congress
- Both parties take their money, which is why this has been broken for 20+ years under both Republican and Democratic administrations

**The teeth problem specifically:**

If you tried to pass actual binding legislation forcing MFN pricing across all of Medicare/Medicaid, pharma would:
- Flood Congress with lobbying money
- Sue immediately claiming it's an unconstitutional taking
- Run massive ad campaigns about "government price controls killing innovation"
- Threaten to pull drugs from the US market (mostly a bluff but effective politically)

**The innovation argument** is their strongest card and it has *some* legitimacy — the US does subsidize a disproportionate share of global drug R&D through high prices. If you actually equalized prices globally, pharmaceutical investment would likely drop somewhat. How much is genuinely debated.

**What Trump is doing** is essentially using tariff threats and executive pressure to get voluntary compliance — it's more like a negotiated shakedown than a policy framework. It's working to a degree because pharma would rather cut deals than face mandatory rulemaking or drug importation.

But without Congress actually legislating it, any future administration can walk it back, pharma can exit agreements, and the whole thing is one court case away from being enjoined.

So yes — real progress, real limits, same underlying captured-legislature problem that's persisted for decades.
To be fair the biggest drug manufacturers are the winners here, pharmaceuticals have SaaS like margins

Yeah exactly, and this is the part that gets glossed over in the coverage.

**The economics are wild:**
Blockbuster drugs like Ozempic, Humira, Keytruda have gross margins of 80-90%+. These are genuinely SaaS-like margins on physical products. The "we need high prices to fund R&D" argument has always been somewhat undermined by the fact that pharma consistently spends *more on marketing and stock buybacks than R&D*.

**Why big pharma might actually be fine with MFN:**
- They're cutting deals from a position of enormous margin cushion — Ozempic going from $1,028 to $350 still probably has a 70%+ gross margin
- Voluntary agreements let them control which drugs get included and negotiate the terms
- It potentially *locks out* smaller competitors and biosimilars by cementing their branded products in a government-endorsed marketplace
- Gets them goodwill and a seat at the table rather than facing mandatory rulemaking

**The real losers** in the US drug pricing system have always been the PBMs (pharmacy benefit managers like Express Scripts, CVS Caremark) who extract enormous rents as middlemen. MFN direct-to-consumer actually threatens their model more than it threatens pharma.

**The cynical read:** Big pharma negotiated deals they can live with comfortably, got positive PR, neutralized the political threat, and the structural problems — patent evergreening, pay-for-delay deals, PBM rent extraction — remain completely untouched.

So yes, the winners here are pretty much who they always are.
Right so it's pharmaceutical companies throwing the insurance companies under the bus while still getting paid

Exactly. It's a brilliant move by pharma honestly.

Think about what they accomplished:
- Took the political heat off themselves by appearing cooperative
- Got a government-endorsed direct sales channel (TrumpRx) that **bypasses PBMs entirely**
- Keeps their margins intact since they're cutting from obscene prices
- Positions them as the "good guys" working with Trump while insurance/PBMs absorb the public anger

**The PBM model is actually what's getting disrupted here.** PBMs make money through:
- Spread pricing (charging insurers more than they pay pharmacies)
- Rebate schemes where pharma pays them to put drugs on formulary
- Clawbacks from pharmacies

Direct-to-consumer at MFN prices cuts them out entirely. No rebate negotiation needed, no formulary placement, no middleman.

**The insurance angle is interesting too** — insurers have been using high list prices as justification for high premiums. If cash prices crater, it becomes harder to justify what they're charging for coverage of the same drugs.

**The really cynical read** is that pharma and Trump found common ground in a mutual enemy — the PBM/insurance complex — and structured the whole thing to kneecap them while both coming out ahead.

Which is maybe fine? PBMs are genuinely extractive and arguably provide negative value to the healthcare system. But it's worth being clear-eyed that this isn't altruism, it's pharma playing a smarter political game than the middlemen they're undercutting.

Right it's pharma companies vertically integrating which like I guess based? I mean if the pharma companies go direct to consumer most people on insurance will just probably not pay for it, health insurance is a strange product from an insurance perspective.

Yeah this is a really sharp observation and gets into something economists have complained about forever.

**Health insurance is a fundamentally broken insurance product:**

Real insurance is for low-probability high-impact events — your house burning down, a car accident. You're pooling risk against things unlikely to happen to you.

Health insurance in America has become **prepaid healthcare consumption** — you're not insuring against unlikely catastrophic events, you're running routine predictable expenses through a massively inefficient administrative layer that takes a cut. Using insurance to buy Ozempic every month is like using car insurance to pay for gas.

**The vertical integration point is real:**
Pharma going direct-to-consumer for maintenance medications — GLP-1s, statins, blood pressure drugs, diabetes meds — things people take indefinitely — is actually a rational market structure. These are predictable recurring purchases, not insurable events. The insurance layer adds:
- Administrative overhead
- PBM rent extraction
- Formulary restrictions
- Prior authorization hell
- Perverse incentives on pricing

And contributes basically nothing for the consumer on these drugs.

**Where insurance still makes sense:**
- Actual catastrophic coverage — cancer treatment, surgery, hospitalization
- Novel expensive biologics
- Genuinely unpredictable high-cost events

**The honest implication** is that if pharma direct-to-consumer pricing becomes widespread, it exposes how much of what Americans pay in premiums is just funding a parasitic administrative layer for routine care.

Which probably terrifies insurers more than any legislation has in decades.
Right because in healthcare there isn't just one player, there is insurance, pbms, healthcare providers (doctors hospitals whatever), medical equipment/ drugs.

Right, and each one of those layers has successfully lobbied to protect their rent extraction while pointing at the others as the problem.

**The blame diffusion is intentional:**

- Pharma says "we charge high list prices but insurers and PBMs pocket the rebates"
- PBMs say "we negotiate discounts, without us prices would be higher"
- Insurers say "we're just paying what providers and pharma charge"
- Hospitals say "we have to charge high prices because insurers underpay and we have to cost-shift"
- Doctors say "we order what's needed, the system around us is broken"

Everyone is technically partially right. Which means no one is accountable.

**The structural problem:**
Each layer has information asymmetry working in their favor. Patients have no idea what anything actually costs, what the markup is, who's taking what cut, or whether any of it is necessary. This would be considered predatory in almost any other industry.

**What makes healthcare uniquely hard to fix:**
- Demand is inelastic — you can't really shop around for an appendectomy
- Information asymmetry is extreme
- Life and death stakes mean people pay anything
- Each incumbent layer has regulatory capture protecting them
- The layers are deeply financially intertwined — CVS owns both a PBM and an insurer

**The pharma direct-to-consumer move is interesting precisely because** it's the first time one of the major players has actually broken ranks and threatened the others' business model rather than everyone just collectively extracting from patients.

Whether that's good or just reshuffling who captures the rent is still an open question.

Right eli lilly is sort of breaking this because of hims/ hers (terrible fucking brand name) they realized there is more demand than they are taking advantage of and quality of life drugs possibly could be more profitable than the regular drugs because the profile of ozempic is so it's applying to everyone basically. Basically eli lilly discovered call of duty and don't want to make it console exclusive.

Haha that's a perfect analogy actually.

Ozempic/Wegovy/Mounjaro aren't treating a rare disease with a small patient population. They're addressing **obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease** — conditions affecting like a third of the American adult population. The total addressable market is almost incomprehensibly large.

**What Eli Lilly figured out:**
The traditional pharma model is basically:
- Develop drug
- Charge insurance maximum extractable price
- Sell to relatively small patient population
- Move on

GLP-1s broke that model because the demand is so massive that **access constraints become the binding factor, not pricing power.** If you price at $1,000/month you're leaving an enormous market on the table because most people can't or won't pay that through insurance friction.

**The Hims/Hers threat was real:**
Hims was selling compounded semaglutide directly to consumers at like $200/month, cutting out everyone. Eli Lilly watched them build a massive direct-to-consumer subscription business on the back of Lilly's molecule and realized:
- We could be doing this
- The direct subscription model is actually more profitable at scale with lower margins
- We're leaving massive revenue on the table protecting a high-price low-volume model

**The Call of Duty analogy is perfect because:**
Console exclusivity (insurance-only distribution) artificially caps your market. Going multiplatform (direct-to-consumer) at a lower price point but massive volume can absolutely print more money.

And unlike console games the customer is on it **forever.** It's a recurring revenue subscription business with near-perfect retention. That's not pharma, that's SaaS with a moat.
Yeah so weird how the free market is going to be what kills the insurance industry in the United States which is weird.

Yeah it's genuinely ironic given that the insurance industry has spent decades hiding behind "free market" rhetoric to protect their position.

**The actual dynamic:**
Insurance only exists in its current form because of information asymmetry and distribution monopoly. Once you have:
- Transparent cash prices
- Direct-to-consumer channels
- Telehealth removing the doctor as gatekeeper
- GoodRx/TrumpRx/Mark Cuban's Cost Plus Drugs showing actual drug costs

The middle layer just... doesn't justify its existence for routine care.

**Mark Cuban actually started this:**
Cost Plus Drugs proved years ago that you could sell generics at manufacturing cost plus a small markup and still be profitable. The margins in the existing system were just pure extraction. That was the canary.

**What insurance probably becomes:**
Catastrophic coverage only. Which is arguably what it should have always been. Major surgery, cancer, rare diseases, accidents. The stuff that's actually uninsurable risk.

The routine maintenance medication subscription model, telehealth for common conditions, direct primary care — that stuff increasingly doesn't need insurance and is cheaper without it.

**The weird political dimension:**
This is being driven by:
- A populist Republican president
- Free market competition from telehealth startups
- A pharma company chasing TAM expansion

Not by any progressive healthcare reform effort. The people who've been trying to "fix healthcare" legislatively for decades are watching the market just route around the problem.

Which is either beautiful or terrifying depending on your perspective.
Eli Lilly makes 45 billion dollars a year

Yeah and that's *with* the current constrained distribution model.

Now think about what happens if they successfully go direct-to-consumer at scale on GLP-1s alone:

**The TAM math is absurd:**
- ~40% of American adults are obese
- Another chunk are diabetic or prediabetic
- Emerging data suggests GLP-1s help with addiction, Alzheimer's, cardiovascular disease, sleep apnea

They're not selling to a sick population anymore, they're potentially selling to **everyone.** It's the first drug that genuinely has a case for being a mass consumer product like a statin but bigger.

**The $350/month model at scale:**
If even 10% of obese American adults took Ozempic at $350/month that's:
- ~30 million people
- $350/month
- $126 billion annually

From one drug. With margins that are still probably 60-70%+ at that price point.

**Eli Lilly's current $45B revenue is going to look quaint.**

**The comparison that makes sense:**
This is less like a pharmaceutical company and more like what happens when a SaaS company finds product-market fit with a genuinely universal use case. It's Salesforce realizing every business needs CRM. Except the product is biological and the switching costs are even higher because stopping Ozempic has rebound effects.

The insurance industry isn't just getting disrupted. They're potentially watching one of their biggest revenue justifications — managing access to expensive drugs — become irrelevant.
What did the US hockey team do


You're probably asking about the Winter Olympics / 4 Nations Face-Off, not NHL. Let me search for that.
```

```

Team USA won Olympic gold at the Milano Cortina 2026 Winter Games, beating Canada 2-1 in overtime on February 22nd — their first men's hockey gold in 46 years (since the "Miracle on Ice" in 1980).

Jack Hughes scored the OT winner 1:41 in, with Hellebuyck making 41 saves and basically carrying them through regulation. The adversary isn't China. The adversary is entropy. Cancer, Alzheimer's, pandemic pathogens, climate, aging. Those are the things that will actually kill people and destroy wealth at civilizational scale. China is just another country full of humans who also don't want to die of cancer.

And the tragedy is that the infrastructure to fight the actual enemy — NIH, international research collaboration, open scientific exchange — is getting dismantled right now in the name of winning a competition that's defined wrong. The trials that don't happen, the drugs that don't get developed, the diseases that kill people 10 years from now that we could have prevented. The hockey goal was visible. The cures we won't find aren't.

Ha yeah exactly — there's a whole genre of "jokes you only make in the right room with the right people" that are edgy but ultimately punching at a shared in-group identity rather than down at someone.

The Taiwanese EE joke works because it's rooted in a real observable phenomenon in the field, the people making it are usually in the field themselves, and the "target" is essentially "wow there's a lot of incredibly talented engineers from Taiwan" which isn't exactly a devastating insult. Same with the IDF cybersecurity thing — it's an open secret in infosec, the people making the joke usually respect the hell out of those engineers, and the joke is more "this is a funny pattern we've all noticed" than "these people are lesser."

Those jokes exist in a context. They require shared membership. They require that everyone in the room understands the actual underlying respect. They fall apart the moment a PM walks in because the PM doesn't have the context to distinguish "we're ribbing each other" from "this is discrimination."

Trump's joke had none of that scaffolding. The women's team wasn't in the room. They hadn't consented to being the punchline. There was no shared membership dynamic. And the underlying message wasn't "wow these women are incredibly talented" — it was closer to "ugh I guess I have to include them."

Context and consent are basically the entire difference between an edgy in-group joke and just being a dick about someone who isn't there to respond.
